text: A sixty-seven-year-old man with a history of low rectal cancer who underwent preoperative radiation and APR and postoperative chemotherapy presented to our institution approximately four weeks after completing chemo with a perineal hernia. He wished to have this repaired and a robotic approach was chosen.

The robotic side cart is positioned to the left of the patient at the foot of the bed. The surgical setup includes a three-arm robotic system with an assistant using a five-millimeter port. A twelve-millimeter camera trocar is centrally placed between two eight-millimeter robotic trocars.
The surgeon reduces the small bowel incarcerated in the hernia, carefully taking down adhesions to mobilize the bowel further. The full extent of the hernia is visualized.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter, visible with a lighted stent, alongside the bilateral iliac vessels. The preperitoneal dissection is initiated on the right side of the pelvic wall.

The surgeon continues the dissection anteriorly towards the patient's left side, creating a peritoneal flap. The flap is then dropped over the hernia defect to obliterate the space and serve as a suspensory membrane for the mesh. Interrupted absorbable sutures are used to secure the flap in place.

The surgeon measures the space for mesh placement and introduces a coated polypropylene mesh marked at the midline. The sacral promontory is identified, and the mesh is anchored using a non-absorbable suture.
The surgeon fixates the polypropylene mesh to the pelvic sidewall, taking care to avoid the ureter and iliac vessels. The mesh is also anchored to Cooper's ligaments on both sides.
The surgeon fixates the mesh to the median umbilical ligament using an absorbable suture, completing the mesh placement in the hernia repair procedure.
The patient did well post-operatively and was discharged home on post-op day five. He's made a full recovery with no complications. Here's a look at his CT one year after surgery.
